title: Cross-Border Guarantees
content:
Session 4: FEM Guarantee Regulations 2000 
SRM Exploration Private Limited v N&S&N Consultants s.r.o. [2012 | DHC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 2 Czech companies where 1 was buying the other ‚Üí SRM (an Indian company) was the guarantor ‚Üí the buyer went into 
winding up and the guarantee was invoked. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč This was a cross-border corporate guarantee issued by an Indian resident in favour of a non-resident. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Nature of Transaction under FEMA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The guarantee related to a foreign transaction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Classified as a capital account transaction under FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SRM argued that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Issuance of the guarantee violated FEMA. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since it was allegedly illegal, the guarantee was unenforceable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Consequently, SRM claimed it was not liable to pay. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the issuance of a cross-border corporate guarantee without RBI approval violated FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč If yes, whether such violation renders the guarantee void or unenforceable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether an arbitral award / claim based on such a guarantee can be enforced in India despite FEMA violations. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 3(b) & 3(d), FEMA prohibits: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Making payments to persons resident outside India. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Entering into financial transactions related to assets abroad. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such acts require RBI permission unless otherwise permitted. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 6(3)(j), FEMA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Governs issuance of guarantees by persons resident in India in favour of non-residents. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Categorised as a capital account transaction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Requires RBI approval. 
185 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA Manual, Para 8.B.1 explicitly states that guarantees issued in favour of non-residents require prior RBI approval. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FERA, 1973, Section 47: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Prohibited contracts that evaded FERA. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Contained express provisions affecting enforceability. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Allowed recovery proceedings despite violation. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA does not replicate this provision. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Existence of a FEMA Violation 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court assumed (without conclusively deciding) that the guarantee may have been issued without RBI 
approval ‚Üí could constitute a technical violation of FEMA. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The critical issue was not whether FEMA was violated, but ‚Üí does a FEMA violation automatically void the 
transaction? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Interpretation of Section 3, FEMA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 3 prohibits certain foreign exchange dealings without RBI permission BUT ‚Üí does not state that 
transactions in violation are void or unenforceable ‚áí FEMA is silent on nullification of contracts. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Legislative Shift from FERA to FEMA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under FERA, 1973, there were explicit provisions impacting contract enforceability. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA consciously omits such provisions ‚Üí reflects a deliberate legislative intent. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Parliament intended not to invalidate transactions merely because they contravene FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Consequences of Accepting SRM‚Äôs Argument ‚Üí allowing parties to escape liability by pleading FEMA violations would: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Undermine contractual certainty. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Send adverse signals to foreign investors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Allow Indian parties to benefit from their own regulatory breaches. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Enforcement of Award 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since the guarantee was not void, claims arising from it were enforceable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA violations, if any, may attract regulatory consequences, but: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Do not bar contractual or arbitral enforcement. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Delhi High Court held that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even if issuance of the guarantee violated FEMA, such violation does not render the guarantee void or 
unenforceable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The absence of a voiding provision in FEMA (unlike FERA) indicates: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A clear legislative intent to preserve contractual enforceability. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Accordingly: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SRM Exploration Pvt. Ltd. was liable to honour the guarantee. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The award/claim based on the guarantee was enforced. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The appeal was dismissed, and SRM was directed to pay. 
 
Videocon Industries Limited v Intesa Sanpaolo [2014 | BHC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Videocon approached Intesa Sanpaolo for a loan of ‚ā¨35 million ‚Üí to finance a step-down subsidiary which was undergoing 
insolvency proceedings in Italy. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The bank agreed to extend the loan on the condition that Videocon would guarantee repayment. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Videocon issued a Patronage Letter in 2007, functioning as a letter of guarantee. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The guarantee undertook that Videocon would ensure repayment if the subsidiary defaulted. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Contractual condition: the subsidiary was required to maintain sufficient balance in its current account ‚Üí defaulted. 
186 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Court of Turin (Italy) passed a judgment against Videocon enforcing the guarantee ‚Üí Intesa Sanpaolo sought 
enforcement in India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Videocon argued that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RBI permission should have been obtained before issuing the Patronage Letter. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since there was a potential outflow of foreign exchange, the guarantee violated FEMA. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Consequently, Videocon should not be compelled to pay, and the Italian judgment should not be enforced. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the issuance of a patronage letter / guarantee by Videocon in favour of its foreign step-down subsidiary violated 
FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether prior RBI permission was mandatory for such a guarantee. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether FEMA guidelines / NDI Rules could: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Prevent Videocon from paying, and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Bar enforcement of the Italian court judgment in India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Videocon could rely on its own regulatory non-compliance as a defence to avoid payment. 
 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 3 ‚Äď Foreign Exchange Management (Guarantees) Regulations, 2000 ‚Üí No person resident in India shall give a 
guarantee or surety in respect of a liability owed by a person resident outside India save as otherwise provided, or with the 
general or special permission of the RBI. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 5(b)(i) ‚Äď Guarantees Permitted ‚Üí An Indian party promoting or setting up a Joint Venture (JV) or Wholly 
Owned Subsidiary (WOS) outside India may give a guarantee to or on behalf of such entity in connection with its business. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Subject to compliance with: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
FEMA (Transfer or Issue of Foreign Security) Regulations. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Such guarantees do not mandatorily require prior RBI approval, provided they fall within permissible 
financial commitments. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 13, FEMA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Provides a penalty regime for contraventions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Indicates FEMA‚Äôs regulatory (not invalidating) nature. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Principle: No Party Can Take Advantage of Its Own Wrong 
 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Nature of the Guarantee 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The patronage letter was, in substance, a corporate guarantee. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč It was given by a resident Indian company, in favour of a foreign step-down subsidiary ‚Üí qualifies as a capital 
account transaction, but not a prohibited one per se. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Applicability of Regulation 5(b) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Guarantees by an Indian parent for its WOS or step-down subsidiary are permissible. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such guarantees are valid so long as they fall within permissible financial commitments of the Indian company 
(turnover, net worth, etc.). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the ‚ā¨35‚Äď38 million exposure exceeded Videocon‚Äôs permissible limits: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Was a question of fact or at most a mixed question of law and fact. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Knowledge of this lay exclusively with Videocon. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Prior RBI Permission 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The phrase: ‚Äúwith the general or special permission of the Reserve Bank‚ÄĚ, does not mean ‚Äúprior permission‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Wherever prior approval is mandatory, FEMA or the Regulations explicitly say so. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Absence of such language means: 
187 
 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Approval may be general, or 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Post-facto. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, no mandatory prior RBI approval was required for the patronage letter. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Post-facto Compliance & Penalty Regime 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even assuming a violation, FEMA provides for penalties under Section 13. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč It does not render the transaction void or unenforceable. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
This reinforces FEMA‚Äôs civil-regulatory framework, not a prohibitory one. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Defence Raised Belatedly 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Videocon never claimed FEMA violation from 2007 until receipt of statutory notice AND raised the defence 
almost four years later, after insolvency proceedings commenced. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such a defence is dishonest and an afterthought. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A party cannot take advantage of its own wrong. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Consequentialist Reasoning & Foreign Investment 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court relied on SRM Exploration Pvt. Ltd. v N&S&N Consultants s.r.o. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Accepting Videocon‚Äôs defence would: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Undermine trust in Indian corporate guarantees. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Send negative signals to foreign lenders and investors. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Discourage cross-border financing involving Indian companies. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Insolvency Context 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The debt was undisputed prior to the statutory notice. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč In winding-up proceedings: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Only if there is a substantial and bona fide dispute will courts refuse enforcement. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Videocon had no arguable defence on merits. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Hence, payment (not mere deposit) was justified. 
 
POL India Projects Limited v Aurelia Reederei GmbH [2015 | BHC | 1 J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč D.B. Shipping LLC entered into a voyage charter party with Aurelia Reederei GmbH, a foreign shipowner, providing for 
arbitration seated in London and governed by English law.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč POL India Projects Limited, an Indian resident company, issued a letter of guarantee in favour of Aurelia, jointly 
guaranteeing performance of the charter party obligations, which the arbitral tribunal treated as a deed of guarantee. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Disputes arose and arbitration was commenced in London, where POL India argued that the guarantee was illegal under 
FEMA and the Foreign Exchange Management (Guarantees) Regulations, 2000, as it was issued without RBI permission. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The arbitral tribunal rejected this defence, held the guarantee valid under English law, and passed a final award 
against POL India for USD 870,059.24 with interest.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Aurelia thereafter sought enforcement of the foreign award in India under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 
1996, which POL India resisted before the BHC. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether enforcement of the foreign arbitral award should be refused under Section 48(2)(b) of the Arbitration and 
Conciliation Act, 1996 on the ground that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The underlying guarantee violated FEMA (Guarantees) Regulations, 2000, and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Enforcement would therefore be contrary to the public policy of India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether prior permission of the RBI was mandatory for issuance of the guarantee by POL India, and if so, whether 
non-compliance renders the award unenforceable. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s47: Conditions for enforcement of foreign awards. 
188 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s48(2)(b): Enforcement may be refused if contrary to public policy of India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Foreign Exchange Management (Guarantees) Regulations, 2000 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 3: Restricts Indian residents from issuing guarantees in favour of non-residents without RBI approval. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 5: Lists permissible guarantees and conditions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Judicial Precedents 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Videocon Industries Ltd. v. Union of India (Division Bench, Bombay HC): interpreted FEMA Guarantee 
Regulations, holding that not all guarantees require prior RBI permission. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Renusagar Power Co. Ltd. v. General Electric Co. (SC): FEMA/FERA violations do not automatically render 
enforcement contrary to public policy ‚Üí cannot be used as a shield to avoid contractual liability. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Interpretation of FEMA Guarantee Regulations 
‚ěĘ‚Äč POL India voluntarily issued the guarantee + acted upon it & allowed the contract to operate for several years + 
raised FEMA objections only at the enforcement stage ‚áí represented to the world that the guarantee was binding. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Public Policy Argument under Section 48 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even assuming (arguendo) that RBI permission was required and such permission was not obtained ‚Üí would not 
amount to a violation of the fundamental policy of Indian law & nor would it be against the interest of India, 
justice, or morality. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA as a ‚ÄúShield‚ÄĚ ‚Äď Rejection 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reaffirmed the Renusagar principle ‚Üí foreign exchange laws cannot be used to avoid payment obligations under a 
valid arbitral award. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA does not invalidate the award; at most, it regulates the mode and timing of payment. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Qualitatively Different Reasoning  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Once POL India entered the commercial arrangement and benefitted from it could not later invoke FEMA to deny 
liability. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Reflects judicial concern for: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Commercial certainty 
‚Ė†‚Äč
India‚Äôs credibility in international arbitration 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Preventing abuse of regulatory statutes 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The foreign arbitral award is enforceable in India ‚Üí cannot be refused under Section 48(2)(b). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Even if prior RBI permission was required (which the Court held it was not), non-compliance does not render the award 
unenforceable ‚Üí does not violate public policy of India.
cases: Videocon Industries Limited v Intesa Sanpaolo
POL India Projects Limited v Aurelia Reederei GmbH
